chunk_summary: This section discusses challenges in outpatient CMAM implementation in India, focusing on escalation defaulting and discordant anthropometry decisions in managing SAM cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the concept of escalation defaulting in outpatient CMAM programs.
Analyze the impact of discordant anthropometry on SAM management decisions.
Evaluate strategies to improve adherence to outpatient treatment protocols in India.
Identify key guidelines from WHO and UNICEF for managing SAM in community settings.
key_facts: Escalation defaulting refers to cases where patients fail to follow up after being referred to higher levels of care.
Discordant anthropometry occurs when weight-for-height and mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measurements provide conflicting classifications of malnutrition severity.
WHO guidelines emphasize the use of MUAC <115 mm or weight-for-height Z-score <-3 SD for SAM diagnosis.
India's CMAM programs face challenges with dropout rates exceeding 20% in some regions.
The NITI Aayog report highlights the need for better community engagement to reduce defaulting rates.
Joint WHO-UNICEF guidelines recommend community-based approaches to improve SAM treatment outcomes.
reference_quote: "WHO guidelines emphasize the use of MUAC <115 mm or weight-for-height Z-score <-3 SD for diagnosing severe acute malnutrition." (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK601654/)
document_key_topics: Outpatient management of SAM in India
